extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A thermal incubator system consisting of an insulated styrofoam box equipped with a heatbed, a fan for air circulation, three DS18S20 high‑precision 1‑Wire temperature sensors, a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B acting as the controller, MOSFETs and optocouplers on a custom PCB to drive the heatbed, and a low‑level driver that interfaces with the sensors and actuators. The system operates in a laboratory environment to maintain a target temperature for biological samples, and its dynamics are modeled with two thermal models (Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real‑time monitoring and visualization of the incubator status via a time‑series database and dashboard; on‑demand calibration of the thermal model (Model B) using automated calibration routines; anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation through a Kalman filter that fuses measured sensor data with the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature; what‑if simulation capability using co‑simulation of the physical twin and the digital twin to evaluate controller parameter changes; and a self‑adaptation loop that detects changes in plant behaviour, schedules experiments, re‑calibrates the model, re‑configures the Kalman filter and controller, and optimizes control strategy.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on a RabbitMQ message broker running on the Raspberry Pi to synchronize communication between the controller, low‑level driver, and the Digital Twin; the controller implements a bang‑bang control strategy; the low‑level driver abstracts sensor/actuator communication and enforces a safe protocol; the Digital Twin uses a Kalman filter for state estimation; Model B (four‑parameter thermal model) is used for simulation and prediction; a time‑series database (e.g., InfluxDB or similar) stores telemetry for real‑time visualization; a dashboard (e.g., Grafana) displays live data; co‑simulation is performed to run what‑if scenarios; all hardware and software components are open‑source and available on GitHub (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real‑time data exchange via RabbitMQ; the low‑level driver periodically samples temperature (frequency not specified) and the controller issues commands; visualization and Kalman filtering operate in real‑time with no explicit latency constraints mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Single Digital Twin instance per incubator; the architecture is centralized with the Raspberry Pi controller coordinating all components; no decentralized or multiple twin instances are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Centralized architecture: Raspberry Pi 4 runs the controller and low‑level driver; RabbitMQ message broker handles communication between controller, driver, and external services; data is streamed to a time‑series database for real‑time dashboards; a Kalman filter estimates unmeasured heat‑bed temperature; co‑simulation and calibration loops run on the same platform to update Model B and controller parameters.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration is achieved through RabbitMQ (AMQP) for inter‑component messaging; no external enterprise systems, other Digital Twins, or interoperability standards are referenced; the system relies on open‑source hardware and software available in the public repository.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation for the incubator comprises two analytical thermal models: Model A (two free parameters – air heat capacity C_air = 616.56 J/K and box conductance G_box = 0.65 J/K) and Model B (four free parameters – C_air = 486.12 J/K, G_box = 0.856 J/K, heater heat capacity C_heater = 33.65 J/K, heater conductance G_heater = 0.87 J/K). These models capture the dynamics of the air inside the insulated box and the heatbed’s thermal inertia. Data sources are the three temperature sensors (T1 outside the box for ambient temperature, T2 inside near the heatbed, T3 inside at the coldest point), the fan voltage (12 V or 18 V), and the heatbed power. Data are collected by the low‑level driver, periodically read from the sensors, and uploaded to the RabbitMQ communication server; from there they are consumed by the controller and stored in a time‑series database for monitoring and visualization. Calibration algorithms run on the Digital Twin side to update the model parameters using the experimental data trajectory; the updated models are then used for simulation, prediction, and anomaly detection (via a Kalman filter). The relationship between the models is hierarchical: Model B extends Model A by adding heatbed parameters, providing higher fidelity for heatbed inertia prediction. The models’ roles include real‑time simulation of temperature evolution, calibration, what‑if scenario analysis, and informing control decisions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a fan and a heatbed. The fan is powered at either 12 V or 18 V (the latter used in experiments to improve temperature uniformity). The heatbed is controlled by a bang‑bang controller implemented on a Raspberry Pi; the controller receives temperature measurements and sends on/off commands to the heatbed via the RabbitMQ communication server. Control parameters include lower limit (LL) and upper limit (UL) for temperature, heating duration (H), and waiting duration (C). The low‑level driver implements a safe protocol to ensure the PT remains in a safe state, preventing over‑heating or fan over‑current. Remote control is achieved through RabbitMQ message queues; the controller listens for command messages and publishes control states. Actuation ranges are limited by the hardware specifications of the fan (12–18 V) and the heatbed power rating (not explicitly specified but bounded by the controller’s on/off logic). Safety constraints are enforced by the low‑level driver’s safe protocol and by monitoring the temperature against LL and UL.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure consists of three thermocouple/temperature sensors: T1 positioned outside the insulated box to measure ambient room temperature, T2 placed inside the box near the heatbed (hottest point), and T3 placed inside the box at the coldest point. Sensors are read periodically by the low‑level driver; the sampling interval is not explicitly stated but is implied to be frequent enough for real‑time control (typically on the order of 1 Hz). Data are transmitted via the RabbitMQ server to the controller and stored in a time‑series database. Accuracy and precision specifications are not provided in the documents. Spatial distribution is such that T2 and T3 capture temperature gradients within the box, while T1 provides a reference for ambient conditions. Data collection is performed by the low‑level driver, which uploads the sensor readings to the communication server for downstream processing.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by selecting Model B for its superior ability to capture heatbed thermal inertia; Model A is used for rough approximations. Validation and verification are performed through calibration experiments that generate temperature trajectories used to fit the model parameters. Uncertainty sources include sensor noise and unmodeled environmental effects; these are mitigated by the Kalman filter used for anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation. Error detection is performed by monitoring deviations between model predictions and sensor data; significant discrepancies trigger anomaly alerts. Quality assurance processes include on‑demand calibration, self‑adaptation loops that re‑calibrate models when new objects (e.g., cold water) alter plant behavior, and periodic re‑optimization of controller parameters via what‑if simulations. The Digital Twin framework thus incorporates continuous validation, calibration, and adaptation to maintain model fidelity and data integrity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data is collected by three DS18S20 1‑Wire digital thermometers. The low‑level driver running on the Raspberry Pi periodically polls the sensors and publishes the readings to a RabbitMQ broker that runs on the same Raspberry Pi. The data is transmitted over the local network using the AMQP protocol and is consumed by the digital twin components such as the controller and analytics modules.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The controller subscribes to command queues on RabbitMQ and sends control messages as JSON objects (e.g., {'fan':'ON'}, {'heatbed':'ON'}, {'heatbed_duration':30}, {'wait_duration':5}). Upon receipt, the low‑level driver validates the command format, enforces a simple safety protocol (ensuring temperature limits are not exceeded), and forwards the command to the corresponding actuator via GPIO (through MOSFETs and optocouplers). The driver publishes an ACK message back to the controller. Conflict resolution is handled by the controller’s bang‑bang state machine, ensuring only one actuator is active at a time. Authentication and encryption are not employed in the current implementation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All components communicate over a local Ethernet/Wi‑Fi network to a RabbitMQ broker running on the Raspberry Pi. The broker uses the AMQP protocol (RabbitMQ default). The network topology is a simple star with the Pi as the central broker. No TLS or authentication is configured. The broker provides at‑least‑once delivery semantics for the messages.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital twin runs entirely on the Raspberry Pi 4 Model B. The Pi hosts the RabbitMQ broker, the controller application, and the low‑level driver. The system is deployed as a set of processes on the Pi, each running as a user‑space service. No external cloud or edge servers are used. Availability is limited to the uptime of the Pi; no redundancy or failover is described.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Construction of the physical twin involved building a thermal incubator with an insulated styrofoam box, a heatbed, a fan, a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B controller, DS18S20 temperature sensors, MOSFET drivers, optocouplers, and a custom PCB. The software stack consists of a RabbitMQ communication server, a low‑level driver that reads sensor data and issues actuator commands, and a controller implementing a bang‑bang (on‑off) temperature regulation strategy. Two simulation models were developed: Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters; Model B better captures the heatbed’s thermal inertia. Calibration of Model B is performed on‑demand via the procedure described in section 3.3, and the calibrated model is used in a Kalman‑filter based anomaly detector and heat‑bed state estimator. Real‑time data from the PT are stored in a time‑series database and visualised on a dashboard. What‑if simulations are executed through co‑simulation of the PT and DT, enabling optimisation of controller parameters. A self‑adaptation loop automatically detects behavioural changes, schedules experiments, re‑configures the controller, re‑calibrates the model, and updates the Kalman filter and simulation parameters. All hardware, software, and datasets are released as open source on GitHub (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example-incubator).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT delivers several decision‑support capabilities: (a) real‑time monitoring – sensor data is streamed to a time‑series database and visualised on a dashboard; (b) anomaly detection – a Kalman filter running on Model B flags deviations between predicted and measured temperatures, providing a confidence interval; (c) state estimation – the filter estimates the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature; (d) on‑demand calibration – an automated calibration routine re‑estimates model parameters when new data is available; (e) what‑if simulation – a co‑simulation engine runs scenarios with altered controller parameters or environmental conditions to predict outcomes; (f) self‑adaptation – upon detecting a new plant (e.g., a bucket of cold water), the DT schedules an experiment, re‑calibrates, re‑optimises controller settings, and updates the controller, thereby closing the loop. Decision making is performed by both automated algorithms (e.g., fault detectors, optimisers) and human operators who review alerts and approve controller changes.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Safe protocol incorporated in the low‑level driver to maintain device safety. The driver periodically reads temperature data, checks for controller commands via RabbitMQ, and uploads data to the communication server. No explicit cybersecurity protocols, authentication, encryption, or threat mitigation mechanisms are described. There is no mention of access control, fail‑safe mechanisms beyond the safe protocol, risk assessment, or safety‑critical system considerations.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. The fan airflow is about 0 _._ 02m [3] s _[−]_ [1] . With a box volume of 0 _._ 03m [3], the fan should take 2 seconds to move all the air within the box. **2.1.4** **The Raspberry Pi** A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (see fig. 3(d)) was selected to implement the controller. The General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) connectors on the Raspberry Pi can be used to connect many accessories such as temperature sensors, fan, and the heatbed. Furthermore it supports wireless internet out of the box, with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, 5 which simplifies deployment of the controller. **2.1.5** **The Temperature Sensor** It is necessary to get feedback from the temperature sensors for feedback control. To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. And each DS18S20 has a unique 64-bit serial code, which allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus. Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
